scope: Re-audit of v9 — coder addresses security v1 S1/S2/S3 on HttpPath (AllowAutoRedirect=true SSRF, X-Signature cross-origin leak, query-string secret persistence). v2 covers the v9 delta (a4a6e5f00..e3309e136): http/this.cs (+122 lines: redirect rewrite + AuthorizationHint) and path/this.Authorize.cs (+16 lines: hint hook). Carry-forwards (F1/F2/F4) re-verified unchanged at HEAD.

build.cs_tests: 2914/2914 pass, 0 fail, 0 skip (TUnit on .NET 10 via `dotnet run --project PLang.Tests`)

attack_surface.exposure: AllowAutoRedirect=false on the static client. Send -> SendWithHops -> FollowRedirect on 3xx. Each hop builds a fresh @this(target.ToString(), Context), calls AuthGate(verb) so the user sees and consents to the new URL, re-signs with the new destination's URL, and recurses. Hop cap MaxRedirectHops=5; scheme allowlisted to http/https; RFC 7231 §6.4 method/body downgrade on 303.
attack_surface.trust_boundary: Remote origin per hop. The user is the gate — Authorize prompt is the trust signal.
mitigations: Fresh HttpPath per hop ensures Permission.Find / Add operate on the new URL
Per-hop SignRequest binds the X-Signature envelope's url claim to the new destination — prior hop's signature never reaches a different origin (S2 cross-origin shape)
Scheme allowlist rejects file://, gopher://, ftp:// etc. with UnsupportedRedirectScheme/400
Hop cap protects against loops and exhaustion regardless of consent
Verb threaded through Send so the new hop is re-Authorized for the same verb the original call started with
gaps: S4 (NEW, Low) — Absolute (the URL shown in the per-hop consent prompt and persisted as the grant key) is built from _uri.Host (Unicode IDN form, homograph-spoofable) and omits _uri.UserInfo. The user's view of the URL diverges from what is fetched and from what gets stored as the grant key.

attack_surface.exposure: path.@this defines virtual AuthorizationHint(verb) returning empty by default; this.Authorize.cs calls it in the prompt loop and appends to the y/n/a choices. HttpPath overrides to emit a one-line constant warning when _uri.Query is non-empty.
attack_surface.trust_boundary: Human-readable terminal output to the user.
mitigations: Hint string is a constant — no URL content interpolated, so no injection surface
Suppressed cleanly when Query is empty or trailing-? only

findings.vector: AllowAutoRedirect=true silently followed 3xx to anywhere from a single user-consent.
findings.fixed_in: v9 — http/this.cs:51-58 (AllowAutoRedirect=false), :320-387 (SendWithHops + FollowRedirect with per-hop AuthGate, scheme allowlist, hop cap, RFC 7231 303 downgrade). Verified by HttpPathRedirectTests.

findings.vector: X-Signature signed against the original URL and forwarded on every redirect hop / sent to any origin.
findings.fixed_in: v9 cross-origin shape closed — http/this.cs:381-386 + :396-422. Each hop builds a fresh HttpPath whose SignRequest reads _uri.ToString() for the envelope's url claim. Verified by signature-decode assertion in HttpPathRedirectTests.
findings.open_residual: Destination-pinned signing envelope verified host-side is still signing-module work — same shape as filesystem-permission F1. Deferred to a signing/* change.

findings.vector: On `a`, full URL (query string included) persisted to sqlite with no user warning.
findings.fixed_in: v9 — virtual AuthorizationHint on path.@this (:96-100 on HttpPath override) renders a one-line warning into the consent prompt when a query string is present. HttpPathPromptHintTests covers present/absent/trailing-? cases.

findings.vector: HttpPath.Absolute (PLang/app/types/path/http/this.cs:115-151) is the only URL shown in the per-hop consent prompt and is also the Permission grant key. Two divergences from what is fetched: (a) `_uri.Host.ToLowerInvariant()` renders an IDN-encoded host in its Unicode form — a Cyrillic-`а` `аpple.com` is visually indistinguishable from `apple.com` in the prompt; (b) `Absolute` builds `scheme://host[:port]/path?query` and silently omits `_uri.UserInfo`, so a redirect to `https://attacker:pwd@victim.example/` displays as `https://victim.example/`. The fresh HttpPath constructed via `target.ToString()` preserves the userinfo on its `_uri`.
findings.preconditions: Attacker controls a previously-consented origin and issues a 3xx whose Location uses an IDN homograph host or embeds userinfo. User accepts the per-hop consent prompt — the entire v9 mitigation relies on the user reading the prompt.
findings.impact: Consent-contract integrity: what the user sees ≠ what is fetched ≠ what is stored as the grant key. (a) IDN homograph spoof bypasses the user's read of the prompt. (b) Userinfo-bearing URLs are persisted as their userinfo-free Absolute form, so once the user grants `https://victim.example/path`, every future `attacker:pwd@victim.example/path` matches the cached grant. .NET does not auto-Basic from userinfo, so no direct credential send.
findings.exploit_sketch: 1. Lure user-consent to https://api.partner.com/x (legitimate). 2. partner.com is later compromised. 3. Origin returns 302 Location: https://%D0%B0pple.com/login (Cyrillic а). 4. User sees: `Allow X to read https://аpple.com/login? (y/n/a)`. 5. User reads `apple.com`, answers `y`. 6. PLang fetches xn--pple-43d.com, returns body to program.
findings.proposed_fix: Render Absolute via `_uri.IdnHost` (punycode) instead of `_uri.Host`. Punycode is visibly distinct (`xn--…`). Either strip `_uri.UserInfo` at construction (recommended) or include it in `Absolute` so the user sees the credentials in the prompt and the grant key.

findings.vector: Actor.Permission.VerifySignature trusts signing.verify integrity without pinning signer identity.
findings.carried_from: filesystem-permission v1/v2 F1 — re-verified at v9 HEAD, unchanged.

findings.vector: permission/this.cs:127 — null-signature row short-circuits to trust.

observations.note: Pre-existing vulnerable NuGet packages (SixLabors.ImageSharp 2.0.0 incl. one high CVE, HtmlSanitizer 9.0.884, MimeKit 4.10.0). Pre-dates this branch — separate dependency-hygiene pass.
SignRequest exception filter swallows OperationCanceledException; cancellation during signing still ships the request unsigned. Tiny; tighten or document.
HttpContent reuse across 307/308 hops (`nextContent = nextMethod==GET ? null : content`) re-passes the same HttpContent instance — single-send semantics; reliability bug not a security finding.

Cached Permission grant for a host previously consented to (for some legitimate direct use) silently re-approves a redirect that lands on that host. Design tension — not a bug under the consent model — but worth a doc note.
Out-of-scope: code.load + [PathScheme] — same Stage-4 concern from v1.
